title: Tea on Silk
stainChemistry: Tea stains contain theaflavins (golden-orange) and thearubigins (dark red-brown) — both are oxidized catechin polymers formed during tea fermentation. These polyphenols hydrogen-bond aggressively to silk's fibroin peptide backbone and form hydrophobic interactions with amino acid side chains. Tea has higher tannin concentration than coffee (3–10% by weight vs. coffee's 1–2%), making it a stronger staining agent despite its lighter initial appearance. Tea also complexes with iron in hard water to form dark blue-black iron-tannate deposits that require different chemistry entirely. Silk's pH tolerance of 4.0–7.0 limits treatment to acidic or neutral agents only.
whyThisWorks: Cold flush prevents thearubigin oxidation — once oxidized, these polymers cross-link with silk protein and become essentially permanent. Diluted Tannin-Go's acetic acid protonates the phenolic hydroxyl groups on theaflavins and thearubigins, breaking their hydrogen bonds to silk fibroin without exceeding silk's pH ceiling. Multiple mild acid cycles are more effective and safer on silk than a single aggressive treatment. The NSD surfactant assists by emulsifying the larger thearubigin polymers. If the stain appears blue-black rather than golden-brown, it's an iron-tannate complex from hard water — acidic treatment alone won't work; citric acid chelation is needed.

spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle flush from back of silk through white towel
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold (60–65°F) — heat sets thearubigins permanently on silk protein
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on clean white towel. Flush cold distilled water from back — use spray bottle at low pressure. Use distilled water specifically: tap water contains iron and calcium that complex with tea tannins to form permanent dark marks. Blot gently — silk loses 20% strength when wet. Check towel for dye transfer from silk before proceeding. If the tea stain appears blue-black instead of golden-brown, suspect iron-tannate complex from hard water.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go (diluted 1:3 with cold distilled water for silk)
spottingProtocol.technique: Cotton swab application from outside in, gentle tamp
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — no steam on silk during tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 2–3 minutes per cycle, up to 3 cycles
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute Tannin-Go 1:3 with cold distilled water. Apply with cotton swab from outer edge inward to prevent ring formation. The acetic acid disrupts theaflavin and thearubigin hydrogen bonds to silk fibroin. Tamp gently with cotton pad — no brush, no spatula on silk. Flush with cold distilled water after 2–3 minutes. Repeat up to 3 cycles. Tea's higher tannin concentration means more cycles may be needed than for coffee.

spottingProtocol.agent: Citric acid solution 1% (for blue-black iron-tannate stains only)
spottingProtocol.technique: Chelation treatment if stain appears blue-black
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if stain is blue-black (indicating iron-tannate complex from hard water): dissolve 1/4 tsp citric acid per cup cold distilled water. Apply with cotton swab. The citric acid chelates the iron ions, releasing the tannin-iron complex. Citric acid is C-rated on silk — very brief contact, 2 minutes max, then flush immediately with cold distilled water. If stain is standard golden-brown tea, skip this step entirely.

spottingProtocol.technique: Follow-up acid + surfactant for thearubigin polymers
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix white vinegar 1:1 with cold distilled water, add 2 drops NSD (nonionic formulation for silk safety). The surfactant helps emulsify the larger thearubigin polymers that resist pure acid treatment. Apply gently with cotton pad, tamp lightly. Flush with cold distilled water. Nonionic surfactants are A-rated for silk.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 3% (drugstore strength — maximum safe for silk)
spottingProtocol.technique: Brief oxidation for residual brown thearubigin color — last resort
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — NO heat, NO ammonia on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only if brown staining persists after 3 acid cycles. Apply 3% peroxide with cotton swab to stain only. NO ammonia — alkaline and damages silk. NO heat activation. Silk is D-rated for peroxide — brief contact only. Watch for 2–3 minutes. Flush immediately with cold distilled water. Follow with vinegar rinse to re-acidify silk. One application only.

homeSolutions: Fresh spill: Blot immediately — never rub silk. Flush with cold distilled water (not tap — hard water minerals darken tea stains). Apply white vinegar diluted 1:1 with cold distilled water, blot gently for 2 minutes, rinse. Tea's higher tannin concentration means act faster than you would for coffee.

Set stain: Soak in cold distilled water with 2 tablespoons white vinegar per quart for 30 minutes. Blot gently. If brown remains, apply single round of 3% peroxide to stain area only — test on hidden seam first. Rinse after 2 minutes. Do NOT use OxiClean on silk.

When to stop: If tea stain has turned dark brown or blue-black and vinegar + 3% peroxide haven't removed it after one cycle, stop. The thearubigins have polymerized beyond home chemistry, or iron-tannate complexing from hard water has occurred. Take to a silk specialist cleaner.
materialWarnings: NEVER use protease enzymes on silk — silk IS protein fiber; protease digests it
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys silk protein, irreversible yellowing
NEVER use ammonia or alkaline spotters — silk degrades above pH 7.0
NEVER use hot water or steam — sets thearubigins permanently AND weakens wet silk
NEVER use OxiClean (sodium percarbonate) — alkaline + oxygen damages silk protein
NEVER use tap water for flushing tea stains — iron and calcium in hard water complex with tea tannins to form permanent blue-black marks
NEVER rub wet silk — tensile strength drops 20%

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go
professional.use: Diluted 1:3 for silk — primary theaflavin/thearubigin bond breaker
professional.note: Acetic acid base within silk's pH range. Multiple mild cycles preferred over one aggressive.

professional.name: Street's Tannin Remover
professional.use: Alternative tannin spotter — dilute 1:4 for silk
professional.note: More concentrated; needs greater dilution for silk safety. Effective on tea's higher tannin load.

professional.name: Citric acid 1% solution
professional.use: Chelation for iron-tannate blue-black stains from hard water tea
professional.note: C-rated on silk — very brief contact (2 min max), flush immediately

professional.name: NSD (Nonionic formulation)
professional.use: Surfactant assist for thearubigin polymer emulsification
professional.note: Nonionic NSD is A-rated for silk — pH neutral, gentle

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar (5% acetic acid)
consumer.use: Dilute 1:1 with cold distilled water — primary tannin spotter
consumer.note: Safest effective treatment for tea on silk at home. Use distilled water to avoid hard water minerals.

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: Last resort for brown thearubigin residue — test first
consumer.note: One brief application only. Rinse after 2 minutes. Follow with vinegar.

consumer.name: Carbona Stain Devils #1
consumer.use: Consumer tannin spotter — test carefully on silk
consumer.note: Formulated for coffee, tea, wine. Check label for silk compatibility.

escalation.when: Brown thearubigin staining persists after three acid cycles + one peroxide application, or blue-black iron-tannate complex present, or silk shows any texture change
escalation.whatToTell: This is tea stain on silk — likely polymerized thearubigins or iron-tannate complex. I used cold distilled water, diluted acid, and brief 3% peroxide only. No alkali, no enzymes, no chlorine. The fiber is undamaged but the tannin chemistry has exceeded home treatment.
escalation.specialistType: Silk specialist dry cleaner — GreenEarth or Solvon K4 preferred (both A-rated for silk). They have professional tannin formulas with extended dwell capability and can test for iron-tannate complexing.

solventNote: Pre-spot wet side with diluted tannin formula before dry cleaning. GreenEarth and Solvon K4 are A-rated for silk. Hydrocarbon also A-rated. PERC is C-rated (odor, hand-feel). For tea's higher tannin load, extended professional tannin formula dwell on the spotting board may be needed. If blue-black color persists, professional may use oxalic acid at reduced concentration for iron chelation — test carefully on silk (D-rated, brief contact only).
